title: The discriminant and sketching quadratic graphs

screens.prompt: A different stretch of trail is modelled by $h(x) = 2x^2-4x+5$ (height above the marker post, in metres, at position $x$ km). Does this stretch ever return to the post's exact height, $h(x)=0$, anywhere?
screens.reveal: Hold that thought. You can tell how many times a quadratic reaches zero without solving it at all, using a single expression called the discriminant. By the end of this lesson you'll be able to check this prediction directly.

screens.prompt: Inside the quadratic formula sits $b^2-4ac$, the discriminant. If $b^2-4ac>0$, what does that tell you about how many times the model $y=ax^2+bx+c$ crosses height $0$?

choices.text: It crosses twice, at two distinct positions
It touches zero exactly once, without crossing
choices.feedback: That's the case when the discriminant equals $0$ exactly, not when it's positive.
That's the case when the discriminant is negative - a positive discriminant means real, distinct roots do exist.

screens.successFeedback: Right - a positive discriminant means two distinct real roots: the curve crosses zero twice.
Right - the discriminant is $-24$, which is negative. That already tells you this stretch never reaches height $0$, confirming your prediction without solving anything further.

screens.body:
When the discriminant equals exactly zero, the trail just touches height $0$ at a single position rather than crossing it. For $h(x)=x^2-6x+9$:

$$
b^2-4ac=(-6)^2-4(1)(9)=36-36=0.
$$

Factorising confirms it: $x^2-6x+9=(x-3)^2=0$, so $x=3$ is a repeated root - the trail just grazes height $0$ at $x=3$ km rather than crossing through it.
screens.selfExplainPrompt: Why does a repeated root mean the trail touches height $0$ without crossing it, rather than passing straight through?
screens.selfExplainAnswer: A repeated root means both 'copies' of the root sit at exactly the same position, so the two crossing points from the discriminant-positive case have merged into one. Graphically, that happens exactly at the very bottom (or top) of the parabola: the curve touches the x-axis at its turning point and turns back the way it came, rather than crossing through to the other side.

screens.worked:
The discriminant also finds unknown coefficients. Suppose a stretch is modelled by $h(x)=x^2+kx+9$ and it's known to have a repeated root (it just touches height $0$ once). Setting the discriminant to zero:

$$
k^2-4(1)(9)=0 \implies k^2=36.
$$
screens.prompt: So $k=\pm6$. What is the positive value of $k$?
screens.successFeedback: Right - $k=6$ (or $k=-6$, which also gives a repeated root).
hints: The positive square root of $36$ is $6$.

screens.prompt: Back to the stretch $h(x)=x^2-2x-3$: drag the point along the curve until the tangent line is as flat as possible, a gradient reading of $0$. That flattest point is the trail's turning point.

goal.description: Target: tangent gradient = 0 (the flattest point on the curve).

screens.successFeedback: That's it - the tangent is flat right at $x=1$, the bottom of the dip in this stretch of trail.
hints: A gradient of $0$ means the tangent is horizontal - look for the lowest point of the dip.
At $x=1$, the curve reaches its minimum value of $-4$.

screens.prompt: The flattest point you just found, at $x=1$ where $h(x)=-4$, has a special name. What is it called?

choices.feedback: The $y$-intercept is where the curve crosses $x=0$, i.e. $h(0)$ - a different, fixed point from the flattest point of the curve.
A root is a position where $h(x)=0$ - the turning point here is at $h(x)=-4$, not a root.

screens.successFeedback: Right - the turning point is where the curve changes from decreasing to increasing (or vice versa), and the tangent there is exactly horizontal.

screens.body:
Not every trail stretch curves upward. A hill-crossing stretch is modelled by $h(x)=-x^2+2x+8$. Completing the square:

$$
-x^2+2x+8=-(x^2-2x)+8=-\left[(x-1)^2-1\right]+8=-(x-1)^2+9.
$$

Because the coefficient of $(x-1)^2$ is negative, the turning point at $(1,9)$ is a maximum, not a minimum, the highest point as you cross the hill. Factorising confirms the roots: $-x^2+2x+8=-(x-4)(x+2)$, so the trail meets height $0$ at $x=4$ and $x=-2$.
screens.selfExplainPrompt: Why does the sign of the coefficient in front of $(x-p)^2$ decide whether the turning point is a maximum or a minimum?
screens.selfExplainAnswer: $(x-p)^2$ is never negative, so adding a positive multiple of it to $q$ can only push the value up from $q$, making $q$ the smallest value reached: a minimum. Multiplying by a negative coefficient flips that: now the term can only pull the value down from $q$, making $q$ the largest value reached: a maximum. The sign of $a$ in $a(x-p)^2+q$ is exactly the sign of the coefficient of $x^2$ once you expand back out.

screens.worked: A different hill-crossing stretch is modelled by $h(x)=-x^2+4x+5$. Factorising: $-x^2+4x+5=-(x^2-4x-5)=-(x-5)(x+1)$, so roots at $x=5$ and $x=-1$. Completing the square: $-x^2+4x+5=-(x^2-4x)+5=-\left[(x-2)^2-4\right]+5=-(x-2)^2+9$, so the turning point is at $x=2$.
screens.prompt: What is the height $h$ at that turning point, the maximum height of this hill?
screens.successFeedback: Right - the maximum height is $9$ m, at $x=2$.

screens.prompt: Back to the opening stretch, $h(x)=2x^2-4x+5$, whose discriminant you found was $-24$ (never reaching height $0$, as you correctly predicted). Completing the square, $2x^2-4x+5=2(x-1)^2+3$. What is the minimum height of this stretch, at its turning point?
screens.successFeedback: Right - the minimum height is $3$ m, always above the marker post. That's exactly why this stretch's discriminant is negative: even at its lowest point it never reaches height $0$. Next lesson uses this same picture, above or below zero, to solve quadratic inequalities.
hints: So the minimum height is just the $+3$ left over.
